notes: DOCUMENTED TARGET — authored from the aider transport + the codex/pi siblings of this scenario; not yet validated against a live recording (aider has no provider auth in this environment, so recording is deferred until auth is available). aider has NO structured AskUserQuestion tool — the user-blocking-question/aider coverage cell (partial/no) is the verdict for that STRUCTURED-tool primitive and does not describe this cell. This cell exercises the adapter-agnostic trailing-? TEXT-classifier path (state_classifier.go decision 2a): the assistant's reply ends in a literal '?', so at turn end IsAgentDone() is true AND the last assistant message ends in '?', and the session transitions working→WAITING instead of working→ready. The load-bearing assertion is the turn_end phase asserting expected_state:waiting. WHY THE CONTRACT HOLDS (assessment yes/yes): aider's parser buffers the assistant prose and, on the `> Tokens:` line, emits an assistant_message whose AssistantText preserves the trailing '?' (pinned by parser_test.go TestParser_TrailingQuestionMark_PreservedForWaitingClassification); the tailer copies that non-empty text into session.LastAssistantText, which IsWaitingForUserInput inspects. The ~1500ms IdleFlush then synthesizes turn_done with EMPTY AssistantText (which does not overwrite the stored question) so the waiting classification survives. TRANSPORT: aider is FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser}; there is no transcript-UUID rollout session — the whole arc lives on the one proc-<PID> session row keyed by the daemon's process scanner (cf. basic-turn spec). RECORDED IN INTERACTIVE MODE (drive-aider-interactive.sh: tmux REPL), NOT headless `aider --message`: in headless mode aider answers and the process exits at turn end, so the working→waiting episode is lost to a process_exited race (cf. codex/pi headless note). The interactive driver keeps the REPL alive after the turn — torn down only at script end via tmux kill-session (no Ctrl-D / /exit) — so the waiting state is observable and dwells; NO reply is sent to the question. SESSION-IDENTITY WORKAROUND (same as pi/multi-turn-conversation and the spec/SKILL.md proc-<PID> pitfall): the live REPL may surface a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch, so session_birth — which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s of launch' — legitimately matches that pre-session. The TURN ARC must land on the bindable session: turn_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working in the whole recording (the pre-session never goes working); pid_bind, turn_end, waiting_dwell and teardown then pin to turn_start's session via same_session_as so a co-occurring pre-session can never satisfy them. TIMING (assessed observes:partial carve-out for the edge): the working→waiting settle lands a few seconds after the last transcript line — gated on the ~1500ms IdleFlush turn_done clamped to the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh tick — so the trailing sleep must be ≥6s to let the waiting episode materialize before teardown; that is a latency delay, not a correctness gap, so the arc is still asserted. HOW RUNS DIFFER: exact assistant question wording, per-turn token counts, PID, and sub-second timing all vary (model-output variance). Stable structurally: one ready→working→WAITING arc on one proc-<PID> session that does NOT settle back to ready before teardown, a single process_exited, and a final waiting state.

text: A session appears in ready within 2s of REPL launch
Prompt asking for one clarifying question → working (binds the bindable session; a transient pre-session never enters working)
The assistant reply ends in a question mark, so the turn settles working→WAITING (the trailing-? text path) rather than working→ready — the agent is waiting for the user to answer
No reply is sent; the session dwells in waiting — the episode is visible and does not skip over or settle back to ready while the agent is gated on the user
The driver tears down the REPL (tmux kill-session) while the session is still waiting → a single process_exited on the session
